messages.content: How does Spain Pick and Roll work?

The Spain Pick and Roll (PNR) is a strategic offensive concept designed to create spacing and scoring opportunities by leveraging the movement of the ball handler, the roller/popper, and the surrounding perimeter players. Here’s how it works based on the playbook details:  

1. **Role Distribution**:  
   - **Driver (Ball Handler)**: Typically takes 20% of PNR scoring opportunities. The driver aims to create space by pausing (hesitation) or using a "KOB" (keep on ball) move to buy time for the back-screen.  
   - **Roller/Popper**: Also accounts for 20% of scoring. The roller moves toward the basket after the pick, while the popper may reset the ball to create a new opportunity.  
   - **Other Perimeter Players**: Account for 60% of PNR scoring. These players react to defensive tags and rotations, exploiting gaps created by the initial pick-and-roll.  

2. **Key Mechanics**:  
   - **Back-Screen Creation**: The ball handler pauses or uses a KOB to delay the defense, allowing the back-screen (from the roller/popper) to generate space for the driver or another shooter.  
   - **Defensive Pressure**: The Spain PNR emphasizes creating space to counter defensive containment, where defenders might "tag" the pick-and-roll and "X out" (close out) on the roller.  
   - **Rejection and Movement**: The play prioritizes rejecting the defense’s help and using the back-screen to get downhill (toward the basket) for scoring chances.  

3. **Origins**:  
   The concept emerged from a game scenario where the 5-man unit got stuck in the middle, leading to a natural PNR setup. This accidental situation highlighted the need for structured PNR execution to maximize spacing and scoring.  

In practice, the Spain PNR relies on precise timing, player movement, and reading defensive rotations to exploit gaps and create high-percentage shots for the driver, roller, or open perimeter shooters.

sources.label: Source 2
sources.text: Spain National Team PNR Numbers = 20% driver coming off pick finishes it, 20% for the roller or popper; 60% for one of the other 3 perimeter players reacting to defensive tags and rotations: This use to be a lot higher for the driver and roller but the numbers have now changedl When looking at PNR, problem they were looking to identify was how can we create more space for the driver and roller? On the two side it's easier for the defense to contain the PNR because they can tag and then X out: The concept came when in a game, the 5 man got accidentally stuck in the middle and fell over; taking out x4 as 4 was rollingl This brought to life the Spain PNR conceptl During Spain PNR, ball handled should look to pause (hesi) or KOB to create time with the back- screen happening and read the situation. Spain gives a great opportunity to reject with the concern over the back-screen and lots of space to get downhill:

messages.content:
Here’s another play from the playbook: the **"Blind Pig"** in a 2-3 set.  

**Setup:**  
- Start with a 2-3 set (players at the top, two on the weak side, three on the strong side).  
- #2 (the post player) is the primary target, but the defense overplays him, forcing him to drift left.  

**Movement:**  
- #4 (the weak-side wing) cuts toward the free-throw line area, positioning himself to receive a pass.  
- #1 (the ball-handler) passes to #4, who then passes to #2 (the post) for a layup.  
- If #4 is denied, #1 can skip the ball to #3 (the strong-side wing) or #5 (the weak-side post) for an alternative option.  

**Purpose:**  
This play exploits overcommitment by the defense on the post. By forcing #2 to move laterally, it creates space for #4 to cut to the free-throw line. The key is quick timing and cutting to the basket. Coaches often use this to break down pressure in the 2-3 set.  

Let me know if you’d like adjustments for specific defenses!

sources.label: Source 1
sources.text: #4 and #5 set a double screen as #2 sets cross-screen for #I. #1 cuts to the low post for possible pass from #3. #2 then comes off double screen set by #4 and #5. This play is commonly called <America 's play" because SO many teams run it. Despite its popularity, it can be a very effective play especially when a team has been running the Flex and is not prepared defensively for this play option: High-Low Option from the Weakside:

sources.label: Source 2
sources.text: #1 makes pass to #2 and cuts through to left corner. #2 passes to #4 stepping out and moves to right corner. #3 makes V cut and receives pass from #4 as #2 makes Flex cut off #5. Flex continuity from 1-3-1 Set: If #2 was not open, he sets Flex screen for #1 as #3 passes to #S coming off downscreen by #4. After his pass, #3 sets downscreen for #2. Another play pattern from the 1-3- 1 Set: #1 passes to #2 and cuts through lane and to the left side: #5 moves in position to take a pass from #2

sources.label: Source 3
sources.text: #2 sets up a little higher: #I passes to #5 who passes to #2 cutting to basket: Skip pass to the offside wing: #4 starts cut to the free throw line vicinity, then dives to opposite post. When #4 is denied, #1 skip passes to #3 as #5 cuts across the lane for possible feed from #3. 2-3 Set 'Blind Pig" Play vs: pressure: #2 is being overplayed so he drifts left. #4 cuts to free throw line and receives pass from #I. #2 cuts and receives pass from #4 for lay-up. Most experienced coaches who have run a 2-3 set have used this play before, one that was popularly called the blind pig:

sources.label: Source 4
sources.text: #2 passes to Post #5 stepping out who relays ball to Post #4 cutting outside. #1 sets screen for #2 who again reads the defense and cuts into the the lane for possible pass from #4. The offense is now in the basic flex as #5 moves down to screen for #I.

sources.label: Source 5
sources.text: 4 scalled play" when a team wants to get the ball inside. #1 advances, passes the ball to #4 and cuts to the right corner: Getting Ball Inside to #5. #4 receives the pass from #1 and reverses ball to #3. #4 cuts away to be in rebound position. #5 cuts across lane fighting for position on his defender. If #5 is open, #3 passes in to the post. Instead of 'reversing the ball to #3,#4 passes the ball to #2 on the strongside of the court Notice that #2, #1, and #5 form a triangle which is a good set to make an inside feed: Here, #2 passes to #1 who can pass inside, or if the defender is on the low side, #1 can return pass to #2 for a feed to #5 on the high side:

messages.content: Trae Young currently holds the lead in assists.

The best play described in the context is the **"High-Low Option from the Weakside"** (also referred to as "America's play"). This play is highlighted as highly effective, especially when a team is using the Flex offense and the defense is unprepared. It involves a double screen set by #4 and #5, with #2 cutting to the low post and creating opportunities for passes or drives. Its popularity and versatility make it a reliable choice.

Another strong play is the **"Best Players in Prescribed Spots"** (from Source 2), which emphasizes using top players (#1, #2, #4) in specific positions to create driving lanes, post feeds, or rebound opportunities. This play is more situational and depends on player strengths, making it adaptable but less universally effective than the High-Low Option.

If the question is about a single, universally effective play, the **High-Low Option** is the clear choice. If flexibility and player strengths are priorities, the "Best Players in Prescribed Spots" play is ideal.

sources.text: BEST PLA YERS IN PRESCRIBED SPOTS Best players are 1, 2, and 4. and are positioned as shown: #1 pases to #5 as #2 does basic flex cut off screen by #4. #I sets downscreen for #4. #5 can pass to #2 if he is open or pass to #4 coming to the top. After #4 receives pass several things may happen according to the strengths of the best players. Examples are in Diagrams 126-128. Being one of the better players, #4 may be an excellent driver and this gives a good opportunity for a drive situation: Notice that #1 has cut to the corner and that #2 has back-screened for #5 to keep defense busy and to enable #5 to get to the offensive board: #2 may be a good back-to-the-basket player and may be guarded by a weaker defender. #1 can flare to the corner, take a pass from #4 and feed #2 in post: If #1 cannot make the feed, he will pass back to #4 for high-low action feed to #2. #5 gets into rebound position while #3 checks back on defense:

sources.text: REMEMBER THIS ABOUT OUT-OF-BOUNDS PLAYS: VIRTUALLY ANY OUT-OF- BOUNDS PLAY CAN LEAD INTO THE FLEX YOU CAN TAKE YOUR FAVORITE OUT-OF-BOUNDS PLAYS AND USE THEM TO GO DIRECTLY INTO THE FLEX

sources.label: Source 4
sources.text: #4 and # 5 _ The Post Players are also interchangeable with both playing on either side of the lane. Good low post players can be kept low on entries to post up while a good shooting post player can be brought outside for good jump shooting opportunities.

sources.label: Source 5
sources.text: #4 passes to #1 cutting to sideline and then sets ballscreen for him: #1 may drive off the screen; shoot, or pass back to #4 rolling to the basket: Remember, 1, 2, and 4 are your BEST players in this situation and YOu can add free lance opportunity for them: They can begin at any of the positions. #4 and #2 could have inverted positions so that #4 would cut off #2s screen. #2 would then come to the top, have the opportunity to drive, pass to #1 for a post feed to #4, or set a ball screen for #1. #2, #3,and #4 are best players: #1 passes to #2 and cuts away leaving #2 in the key flex entry position. #3 cuts crosscourt to the right corner as #4 cuts off #Ss screen for right low block. #2 passes to #3 and makes a UCLA cut off #4. #3 can pass to #2 if #2 is open:
